channel.title: VERTEBRATE.ai — The Weekly
channel.description: VERTEBRATE.ai — the first AI-powered media covering humanoid robots, physical AI, driverless cars, robotaxis and autonomous drones. Daily dispatches and a weekly editorial on the robotics industry.

item.title: The Weekly — 2026-W29
item.description: Figure unveiled its third-generation humanoid, Figure 03, alongside a Helix upgrade that lets robots learn new chores straight from video; NEURA Robotics struck a major chip-and-software alliance with Qualcomm to standardize humanoid 'brains'; and Anduril's autonomous drone fired its first live missile, quietly formalizing a new class of lethal, uncrewed weapons.
item.content:encoded:
Figure unveiled its third-generation humanoid, Figure 03, alongside a Helix upgrade that lets robots learn new chores straight from video; NEURA Robotics struck a major chip-and-software alliance with Qualcomm to standardize humanoid 'brains'; and Anduril's autonomous drone fired its first live missile, quietly formalizing a new class of lethal, uncrewed weapons.

This week the humanoid race shifted from demo-day flourish to something closer to infrastructure. Figure, NEURA Robotics, and Dexterity all made moves toward robots that generalize and scale, while GeniusRobotics claimed a manufacturing first and Anduril's missile test reminded everyone that autonomy's hardest edges are still military ones.

'World's 1st mass-produced humanoid robot' motors to market in China

GeniusRobotics

GeniusRobotics' Ares humanoid robot enters mass production in China, marking the first commercially scaled humanoid platform.

Why it matters. GeniusRobotics claims a manufacturing first that Tesla, Figure, and Unitree have all chased but not yet publicly claimed. The real test isn't the announcement but unit economics and reliability at scale — mass production means nothing if the robots can't hold up outside a showroom.

Read at New Atlas →

New York school district to deploy humanoid robot in classrooms this fall

Salamanca City Central School District, Realbotix

A New York school district is deploying humanoid robots from Realbotix into classrooms this fall to teach STEM subjects.

Why it matters. A public school district putting humanoid robots in front of children is a real-world trust test that no lab demo can substitute for. Watch how Realbotix handles safety oversight and whether teachers actually find the robots useful, or just distracting.

Read at Fox News →

ACE ROBOTICS Unveils Kairos 3.1 and Expands Its Embodied AI Stack from Data to Deployment at WAIC 2026

ACE Robotics

ACE Robotics unveiled Kairos 3.1, an embodied AI system spanning data collection to robot deployment, at WAIC 2026.

Why it matters. ACE Robotics is building a full-stack embodied AI system — data to deployment — that mirrors what Physical Intelligence and Figure are attempting in the US. It's another signal that China's robotics ecosystem is racing to own the entire pipeline, not just the hardware.

Read at Yahoo Finance →

Chinese platform lets people control humanoid robots with thoughts

BrainCo, Nyx He

Chinese brain-computer interface company BrainCo unveiled a platform that lets users control humanoid robots directly with their thoughts via EEG headsets.

Why it matters. BrainCo's brain-computer interface layer for humanoid control points toward teleoperation that skips joysticks and controllers entirely. It's early and gimmicky-looking now, but latency and accuracy improvements here could matter for hazardous or remote work down the line.

Read at Interesting Engineering →

Humanoid robot lands head-high kick during ‘world’s first freestyle’ fighting in China

EngineAI

EngineAI's T800 humanoid robot landed a head-high kick at China's first freestyle robot fighting tournament, continuing to punch even after losing its head.

Why it matters. EngineAI's fighting robot is pure spectacle, but the durability flex — a headless robot still swinging — is a marketing tactic other humanoid makers will feel pressure to match. Expect more staged combat as firms compete for viral proof of ruggedness.

Read at Interesting Engineering →

Tesla's Robotaxi Just Launched in Miami. Here's How Small the Service Area Really Is

Tesla

Tesla launched its Robotaxi service in Miami with a notably constrained operating territory.

Why it matters. Tesla's robotaxi keeps expanding, but a tiny operating footprint in Miami underscores how far the fleet remains from Waymo-scale coverage. The gap between headline launches and actual usable service area is the real story to track.

Read at Yahoo Finance →

World-first airdrop of an autonomous drone boat into rough seas

Kraken Technologies, Capewell, Royal Navy, Royal Air Force, Adam Ballard

The Royal Navy airdropped a Kraken Technologies autonomous drone boat from an RAF cargo plane into rough North Sea waters, testing rapid deployment tactics.

Why it matters. Airdropping an autonomous drone boat from a cargo plane into rough North Sea waters is a real test of rapid-deployment logistics, not a lab exercise. If it works reliably, it changes how navies think about getting unmanned assets into contested waters fast.

Read at New Atlas →

Matternet taps Beeline to accelerate US drone delivery expansion

Matternet, Beeline

Matternet partners with Beeline to scale drone delivery operations across the United States.

Why it matters. Matternet's partnership with Beeline is a quiet infrastructure play to scale drone delivery across the US, competing directly with Wing and Zipline for logistics contracts. Watch how fast regulatory approvals and airspace integration keep pace with expansion ambitions.

Read at DroneDJ →

Anduril Autonomous Drone Fires Its First-Ever Live Missile at a Target

Anduril

Anduril's autonomous drone successfully fired a live missile at a target, marking the company's first weaponized flight test.

Why it matters. Anduril's autonomous drone firing a live missile is the moment lethal autonomy stops being a slide-deck concept and becomes a demonstrated capability. It puts pressure on the Pentagon's procurement pipeline and raises the stakes for how quickly regulators and treaties can catch up.

Read at autoevolution →

A mosquito-hunting killer drone is available for preorder – and I have questions

TechRadar

A mosquito-hunting drone has opened preorders, marking an unusual application of autonomous flight technology to pest control.

Why it matters. A consumer preorder for a mosquito-killing drone shows autonomous flight tech diffusing into oddball civilian niches, well beyond delivery and surveillance. The obvious question is whether it can actually target mosquitoes without misfiring on anything else that moves.

Read at TechRadar →

Autonomous Drone Fleet Manager Clears Pentagon Gate: Tesseract NOMAD Selected

Tesseract

Tesseract's NOMAD autonomous drone fleet management system has won Pentagon selection, a validation of software that coordinates multiple unmanned aircraft in contested environments.

Why it matters. Tesseract's NOMAD software winning Pentagon selection is a reminder that drone warfare's next bottleneck is coordination, not hardware. Whoever controls the fleet-management layer effectively controls how swarms behave in contested airspace.

Read at Tech Times →

US picks containerized drone system for next-gen autonomous warfare

Interesting Engineering

The U.S. military has selected a containerized drone system for deployment as part of its next-generation autonomous warfare infrastructure.

Why it matters. The Pentagon standardizing on containerized drone systems is a bet on modularity — units that can be shipped, deployed, and swapped like cargo. It's infrastructure procurement, less flashy than a missile test but arguably more consequential for how fast autonomous warfare scales.

Read at Interesting Engineering →

Introducing Figure 03

Figure, BotQ

Figure introduced Figure 03, its third-generation humanoid robot redesigned for manufacturing scale and trained on the company's proprietary Helix AI system.

Why it matters. Figure 03 is a redesign explicitly aimed at manufacturing scale, not lab demos, and it's trained on the company's own Helix AI rather than borrowed foundation models. This puts direct pressure on Tesla's Optimus and Unitree to show comparable production-readiness, not just impressive footage.

Read at Figure →

Helix 02 Living Room Tidy

Figure

Figure's Helix 02 robot now tidies living rooms with coordinated tool use and bimanual manipulation, learning new household tasks directly from video without algorithm changes.

Why it matters. A robot that learns new household tasks straight from video, without retraining, is the generalization breakthrough the whole humanoid industry has been chasing. If it holds up outside curated demos, it's a bigger deal than any single hardware upgrade this year.

Read at Figure →

NEURA Robotics and Qualcomm Enter Strategic Collaboration to Advance Physical AI and Cognitive Robotics

NEURA Robotics, Qualcomm

NEURA Robotics and Qualcomm announced a long-term partnership to develop what they call a 'Brain + Nervous System' reference architecture, combining Qualcomm's robotics processors with NEURA's full-stack software to speed humanoid and general-purpose robot deployment.

Why it matters. NEURA and Qualcomm's 'Brain + Nervous System' architecture is an attempt to standardize the chips-and-software stack that every humanoid maker currently builds in isolation. If it catches on, it becomes a real challenge to Nvidia's dominance in robotics compute.

Read at Neura Robotics →

Introducing Foresight

Dexterity, Kevin Chavez

Dexterity unveiled Foresight, its world model for robotic manipulation trained on over 100 million real warehouse actions, designed to let robots reason about physical change and predict outcomes before they happen.

Why it matters. Dexterity's Foresight is a world model trained on 100 million real warehouse actions, aimed at letting robots predict physical outcomes rather than just react. It's a direct competitive shot at Physical Intelligence and Covariant in the race to make warehouse robots reason, not just grasp.

Read at Dexterity →

Wing and Papa Johns partner to build the future of fresh, hot delivery by drone

Wing, Papa Johns, Alphabet, Google Cloud, Kevin Vasconi, Heather Rivera

Wing and Papa Johns are launching a drone-delivery pilot in Charlotte to fly hot sandwiches and pizza to customers, with plans to integrate the service directly into Papa Johns' app and AI ordering system.

Why it matters. Papa Johns betting on drone delivery, integrated into its own AI ordering app, signals fast food is ready to treat drones as a real logistics channel, not a novelty. Charlotte is the pilot; the real story is whether the unit economics survive scaling past one city.

Read at Wing →

Autonomous OGI Drones now Alternative Test Method for EPA Subpart OOOOa, OOOOb

Percepto

Percepto's autonomous drone system wins EPA approval as an alternative method for methane leak detection, letting operators inspect oil and gas sites remotely down to 100 grams per hour.

Why it matters. EPA approval for Percepto's autonomous methane-detection drones is a rare regulatory win that could replace manual inspection crews across oil and gas sites. It's a quiet but concrete example of autonomy actually being adopted for environmental compliance, not just demoed.

Read at Percepto →

item.title: The Weekly — 2026-W28
item.description: 1X gave its Neo humanoid near-human hands and Mitsubishi joined the android race with a 2027 factory plan, while Waymo pushed driverless rides into a half-dozen new cities and Ondas snapped up drone maker DZYNE for $875.8 million.
item.content:encoded:
1X gave its Neo humanoid near-human hands and Mitsubishi joined the android race with a 2027 factory plan, while Waymo pushed driverless rides into a half-dozen new cities and Ondas snapped up drone maker DZYNE for $875.8 million.

The humanoids arrived in force this week, some promising companionship and others promising to replace factory shifts, though nearly every founder spent as much energy managing expectations as raising them. Meanwhile the machines that already work for a living—Waymo's robotaxis and a fleet of autonomous drones from Everest to Tampa Bay—quietly expanded, a reminder that the future often shows up as logistics rather than spectacle.

Humanoid robot designed ‘for a lifetime’ of companionship unveiled

UBTech

UBTech unveiled the U1, a mass-production humanoid robot with emotional AI designed to keep lonely and elderly Chinese people company for life.

Why it matters. Another entrant stakes its claim on the emotional-companion niche, a category more marketed than proven. The gap between a demo that charms and a machine someone loves for years remains vast, and this launch does little to close it.

Read at The Independent →

Human-Level Hands? 1X Just Gave Humanoid Robot Neo Something Close

1X

1X upgraded its Neo humanoid robot with more dexterous hands designed to handle tasks requiring human-level precision and touch sensitivity.

Why it matters. Hands are the hardest part of the humanoid problem, and dexterity is where the vaporware gets separated from the useful. If Neo's new fingers can genuinely fold laundry or handle fragile objects, 1X inches the whole field closer to homes rather than stages.

Read at Forbes →

The first commercial human-like robot is here. Are replicants next?

A startup has shipped its first humanoid robot to customers, raising questions about whether such machines will become servants or competitors.

Why it matters. Shipping a humanoid to paying customers, even a handful, marks the shift from lab curiosity to commercial product. The philosophical hand-wringing about servants versus rivals matters less than the mundane question of what the thing can actually do once it's unboxed.

Read at Fast Company →

This humanoid robotics company is going public, but its CEO isn't promising a robot in your home anytime soon

Agility Robotics, AI2 Robotics, Apptronik, Figure AI, Churchill Capital Corp XI, GXO Logistics, Amazon, Toyota Motor Manufacturing Canada, Schaeffler, Mercado Libre, Peggy Johnson, Michael Klein

Agility Robotics, led by CEO Peggy Johnson, is going public via SPAC merger, becoming the first pure-play humanoid robotics company to trade publicly.

Why it matters. A humanoid company reaching public markets while its own CEO tamps down the home-robot fantasy is a rare moment of candor in a hype-drunk sector. Public investors will now get to price the distance between the promise and the shipping manifest.

Read at TechCrunch →

Mitsubishi joins global robot race, plans to manufacture, deploy humanoid workers in 2027

Mitsubishi

Mitsubishi enters the humanoid robotics race with a plan to manufacture and deploy robotic workers by 2027.

Why it matters. When a $200 billion industrial conglomerate commits to building and deploying humanoids, the field gains manufacturing muscle the startups lack. Mitsubishi's 2027 target puts it in direct competition with Tesla and the venture-funded pack, and factory floors are where these robots have the clearest business case.

Read at Automotive News →

Ex-Tesla Optimus scientist unveils European humanoid robot startup

Tesla

A former Tesla roboticist launches a European humanoid startup, positioning the company between Boston Dynamics' ambition and manufacturing practicality.

Why it matters. The Optimus diaspora continues, with Tesla alumni seeding rival ventures the way PayPal's did a generation ago. A European base is a bet that the continent's factories want a homegrown alternative rather than an American or Chinese one.

Read at Electrek →

Waymo launching robotaxi service in Denver. But only for its own workers.

Waymo

Waymo is quietly testing its Denver robotaxi service on a captive audience of employees before facing actual customers.

Why it matters. Testing on employees lets Waymo gather Denver's altitude-and-snow data without exposing paying riders to the learning curve. It's the cautious playbook that has let the company outlast flashier rivals; the tell will be how quickly the captive audience gives way to the public.

Read at The Colorado Sun →

BayCare Selects Zipline to Build Drone Delivery Network Across Tampa Bay

BayCare, Zipline

BayCare, Florida's largest health system, taps Zipline to deploy autonomous drones for moving blood and medical supplies across Tampa Bay.

Why it matters. Blood and medical supplies are drone delivery's genuine killer app, where minutes matter and roads don't. BayCare's endorsement gives Zipline a marquee health-system deployment and a template other hospital networks will study.

Read at Dronelife →

ZenaTech begins development of sphere-shaped autonomous drone

ZenaTech

ZenaTech is building a spherical autonomous drone, betting that rounded geometry solves problems that rectangular designs don't.

Why it matters. A spherical drone is either a clever answer to durability and safety in tight spaces or a novelty in search of a use case. ZenaTech is wagering that shape is a differentiator; the market will decide whether geometry is a feature or a gimmick.

Read at Investing.com →

Skydio crosses 1,000 drone Dock deployments in just one year

Skydio

Skydio's autonomous charging dock, which enables unattended drone flight, reached 1,000 deployments in one year, validating the company's self-sufficiency strategy.

Why it matters. The dock, not the drone, is the quiet breakthrough: it turns a piloted gadget into an unattended sentry that recharges itself. A thousand deployments in a year suggests the autonomous-inspection market is maturing from pilot projects into standing infrastructure.

Read at DroneDJ →

Beyond the Helicopter: DJI’s EV50 drone brings autonomous logistics to the slopes of Mount Everest

DJI

DJI's heavy-lift EV50 drone is being tested to ferry supplies up Everest's slopes, stress-testing autonomous mountain logistics in real conditions.

Why it matters. Hauling gear up Everest is equal parts marketing and genuine engineering trial, thin air and brutal cold being unforgiving referees. If DJI's heavy-lift drone survives the mountain, the more mundane payoff is autonomous logistics for mines, farms, and construction sites everywhere else.

Read at sUAS News →

Percepto Unveils AI-Powered Inspection Intelligence Platform for Energy Infrastructure and Autonomous Drone Operations

Percepto

Percepto launches an AI-powered inspection platform that lets drones autonomously monitor power grids and critical infrastructure without human pilots.

Why it matters. The value in drone inspection is migrating from the flying to the interpreting, and Percepto is planting its flag on the analysis layer. For utilities managing aging grids, software that flags a failing transformer before it burns is worth more than the aircraft that photographs it.

Read at uasweekly.com →

Pentagon awards $80M task order for AI-enabled tech to defend Air Force bases against small drones

AeroVironment, Jason Idleman

AeroVironment wins an $80.5 million task order to deploy AI-powered drone-detection systems at Air Force bases, part of a broader Pentagon push to fortify installations against small unmanned aircraft.

Why it matters. The $80 million counter-drone order is the flip side of the cheap-drone revolution: every advance in unmanned attack invites an equal spend on defense. Air Force bases are learning what Ukraine's battlefields already proved, that a $500 quadcopter can threaten billion-dollar assets.

Read at DefenseScoop →

Ondas to Buy Military Drone Maker DZYNE for $875.8 Million

Ondas, DZYNE

Ondas is acquiring military drone maker DZYNE for $875.8 million to expand its defense sector presence.

Why it matters. At nearly $876 million, this acquisition is the week's biggest check and a bet that defense budgets will keep favoring unmanned systems. Consolidation is coming to the military drone sector, and Ondas is positioning to be a buyer rather than a target.

Read at Bloomberg.com →

Develop Humanoid Robot Policies End-to-End with NVIDIA Isaac GR00T | NVIDIA Technical Blog

NVIDIA

NVIDIA's Isaac GR00T software pipeline enables training humanoid robots for complex physical tasks without simulation code.

Why it matters. By handing developers a ready-made pipeline for training humanoid behaviors, NVIDIA cements its position as the picks-and-shovels supplier to the entire robot rush. Every startup that builds on GR00T deepens dependence on NVIDIA's stack, the same playbook that made the company indispensable to generative AI.

Read at NVIDIA Developer →

US scientists use Chinese humanoid robot to carry out keyhole surgery

Unitree Robotics

Unitree Robotics' Chinese humanoid robot successfully performed minimally invasive keyhole surgery in a demonstration by American surgeons.

Read at South China Morning Post →

The Safety Data on Autonomous Vehicles

Johns Hopkins researchers examine crash data to evaluate autonomous vehicle safety claims and regulatory gaps.

Read at Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health →

3D-printed interceptor aircraft counters drone swarms at 350 mph

SkyDefense, Nick Verini

SkyDefense's 3D-printed CobraJet interceptor hunts hostile drone swarms at 350 mph, armed with compact guided rounds to replace costly air-defense missiles.

Read at Interesting Engineering →
